seo.pageTitle: From Busan: Gyeongju Ancient Capital Day Tour | AtoC Korea
seo.metaDescription: 10.5-hour small-group Busan to Gyeongju tour: Bulguksa Temple (UNESCO), Ahopsan Bamboo Forest, Gyeongju National Museum, Gyochon Hanok, Woljeonggyo Bridge.
seo.title: 부산 출발 경주 고도 일일 투어 · 여유 페이스 | AtoC Korea
seo.description: 부산 출발 경주 여유 페이스 10.5시간 투어: 아홉산숲 → 불국사(유네스코) → 국립경주박물관 → 교촌한옥마을 → 월정교. 소그룹 밴.

catalog_card.title: 부산 출발 경주 고도 일일 투어
catalog_card.shortCardDescription: 유네스코 레거시 투어와 동일한 코스를 여유 있는 페이스로 진행합니다. 아홉산숲, 불국사, 국립경주박물관, 교촌한옥마을, 월정교까지 — 빠른 일정이 아닌 충분한 체류 시간을 원하는 분께 적합합니다.
catalog_card.subtitle: 여유 있는 페이스와 더 이른 귀환으로 경주 6개 클래식 명소를 하루에.

headlineLine1: 한국 천 년의 수도, 여유 있는 페이스 —

price.priceNote: Small-group price not yet confirmed from operator listing. Comparable Gyeongju-from-Busan tours range $54–90 across packages and operators. Once confirmed, AtoC Korea sale price applies a flat 10% discount.

hero.tagline: 불국사, 박물관, 교촌, 월정교 — 여유 있는 페이스, 더 이른 귀환

galleryItems.alt: Stone bridges and Dabotap pagoda at Bulguksa Temple in afternoon light
galleryItems.caption: Bulguksa — UNESCO World Heritage. Two pagodas (Dabotap, Seokgatap) are National Treasures No. 20 and 21.

galleryItems.alt: Tile-roofed hanok houses along a quiet alley in Gyochon village
galleryItems.caption: Gyochon Hanok Village — the Choi family compound and Gyeongju Hyanggyo Confucian school.

galleryItems.alt: Light filtering through dense bamboo stalks along a forest path
galleryItems.caption: Ahopsan Bamboo Forest — privately preserved by the Mun clan since 1638.

galleryItems.alt: Silla gold artifacts displayed under museum case lighting
galleryItems.caption: Gyeongju National Museum — Silla artifacts including the Cheonmachong gold crown.

galleryItems.alt: Wooden bridge pavilion reflected in calm river water
galleryItems.caption: Woljeonggyo Bridge — 66-meter Silla-style reconstruction completed in 2018.

itineraryStops.name: 부산 픽업 (지하철 3개 역)
itineraryStops.description: 부산 지하철 3개 역에서 순차 픽업: 부산역 1호선(약 08:10), 서면역 1·2호선(약 08:30), 해운대역 2호선(약 09:10). 아홉산숲으로 이동.
highlights: Three pickup options across central Busan (Busan Station, Seomyeon, Haeundae)
Minicoach is small-group (max ~14 pax)
Total transit to Ahopsan Bamboo Forest: ~40 min
**Calm-pace day** — average dwell per substantive stop is ~15 min longer than fixed-itinerary alternatives
Printed bilingual itinerary card distributed at pickup
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Three pickup points cover Busan's three main hotel zones (KTX/Nampo, Seomyeon mid-city, Haeundae beachfront) without forcing guests to transit across the city in the morning. Sequencing west→east minimizes coach distance and lands the group at Ahopsan around 09:50 — early enough that the bamboo forest is at its quietest and most photogenic.

itineraryStops.name: 아홉산숲 (대나무 숲)
itineraryStops.category: Private bamboo forest — K-drama filming site
itineraryStops.description: 아홉산숲은 부산 기장군에 위치한 52만㎡ 사유 원시림으로, 17세기부터 남평 문씨 가문이 9대(약 400년)에 걸쳐 지켜온 맹종죽(중국 왕대나무) 숲과 편백 숲이 공존합니다. 드라마 '대장금' 등 다수 사극 촬영지입니다. 입장료: 성인 ₩5,000 현장 지불. 방문 시간: 약 45분.
highlights: **520,000 m² private forest**, managed by Nampyeong Moon clan **400 years / 9 generations**
**Opened to the public 2016** — relatively new on the Jeju/Busan tour circuit
**Maengjongjuk bamboo corridor at Spot 9** — towering 15–20 m culms
**The King: Eternal Monarch (2020) + Moon Lovers (2016) + 100 Days My Prince (2018)** filming site
**116 protected Korean pine trees over 400 years old** (Joseon-era plantings)
Admission: Adult ₩8,000 / Child ₩5,000
Hours 09:00–18:00, **closed Mondays** (route avoids Mondays)
Calm-pace 75-min dwell lets guests walk the numbered-spot loop without rushing

**Sustainable working bamboo forest** — Nampyeong Moon clan still harvests bamboo for traditional Korean crafts (zithers, baskets, fishing rods); commercial filming requires advance written permission; **Spot 9 + Gutteo Grove + second pine forest** comprise the full numbered loop
timeUsed: Walk to Spot 9 Maengjongjuk bamboo corridor (~15 min)
Photo time + slow walk through bamboo (~25 min)
Return loop past 400-year-old Korean pines (~15 min)
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Ahopsan opens the day with the calmest, most contemplative stop — a meditative bamboo walk before the more architectural Bulguksa midday segment. Selected over the more-famous Damyang Juknokwon (Jeolla province, 4+ hr from Busan) because Ahopsan is **35 min from Busan vs. 4 hr** while delivering a comparable bamboo-forest experience PLUS K-drama filming heritage. **The 9-spot loop pairs naturally with the calm-pace framing** of this tour — guests can take photos at every spot without feeling rushed.

sources: Cultural Heritage Administration of Korea (Nampyeong Moon clan working forest)

itineraryStops.name: 불국사 (유네스코 세계문화유산)
itineraryStops.description: 751년 신라 창건, 1995년 유네스코 세계문화유산 등재. 국보 4점(다보탑·석가탑·연화교칠보교·청운교백운교) 보유. 2023년 5월부터 무료 입장. 방문 시간: 약 60분.
highlights: **UNESCO World Heritage 1995** + **Korea's Historic and Scenic Site No. 1**
Founded **528 CE → expanded 751 CE by PM Kim Daeseong → completed 774 CE**
**Six Korean National Treasures** in a single temple
**Dabotap (NT 20)** = image on Korea's 10-won coin
**Seokgatap (NT 21) + Pure Light Dharani Sutra** = world's oldest extant woodblock print (704–751 CE)
Cheongungyo/Baegungyo (NT 23): 34 steps = the 34 steps to Buddhist enlightenment
**FREE admission since 2023** (Cultural Heritage Protection Act revision)
Hours daily 09:00–18:00, last admission 17:00

**Imjin War 1593 destruction → 1604 partial rebuild → 1969–1973 comprehensive restoration** under President Park Chung-hee — heritage continuity narrative spanning four centuries of war, neglect, and modern conservation
timeUsed: Coach drop-off + walk through pine-lined approach (~10 min)
Birojeon Vairocana Hall + Geuknakjeon Pure Land Hall (~15 min)
Free time + reassemble at coach (~10 min)
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Bulguksa is **the non-negotiable UNESCO anchor of any Gyeongju day tour** and the most-recognized Buddhist heritage site in Korea. The 11:30 arrival is **15 min later than the UNESCO-Legacy sister tour** to allow a more leisurely Ahopsan dwell before the temple — consistent with the calm-pace framing. The 75-min window is calibrated so history-lover guests can complete the full pilgrimage axis without rushing the photogenic Dabotap/Seokgatap pairing in the courtyard. The afternoon arrival window also avoids the morning Korean school-group crowds (~09:30–11:00) that fill the temple courtyards before lunch.

itineraryStops.name: 점심 (경주 현지 식당)
itineraryStops.description: 불국사 출구 인근 현지 식당에서 점심. 경주 쌈밥(경주식 나물 쌈밥 정식), 황남빵 간식 추천. 점심 식사비는 별도(약 ₩10,000~15,000).
highlights: Guide-curated Gyeongju local restaurant near Bulguksa
**Gyeongju-style ssambap** OR **Hwangnam-bbang** (1939 regional pancake)
Vegetarian option available on request
**60-min lunch** — calm-pace framing (15 min longer than fixed-itinerary tours)
Cost ₩12,000–₩18,000 pp, NOT included in tour price
timeUsed: Walk from Bulguksa exit to restaurant (~5 min)
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Lunch is positioned right after Bulguksa to reset before the afternoon's slower-paced museum and hanok village segment. The longer dwell vs. fixed-itinerary alternatives reinforces the calm-pace framing — history-lover guests typically appreciate the breathing room.

itineraryStops.name: 국립경주박물관
itineraryStops.category: National Museum — Silla artifacts (Gold Crown + Emille Bell)
itineraryStops.description: 신라 문명 유물 30만 점 이상 소장. 성덕대왕신종(국보 29호, '에밀레종'), 황남대총 순금 관모, 토제 기마 인물상 등이 대표 유물입니다. 무료 입장. 방문 시간: 약 60분.
highlights: Korea's premier Silla artifacts museum — founded **1945**, main building **1968**, National Museum status **1975**
**100,000+ artifacts** including **30,000+ designated Cultural Heritage items**
**Cheonmachong Gold Crown (NT 188)** — most ornate of 6 Silla crowns, excavated April 16, 1973
**Emille Bell / Sacred Bell of King Seongdeok (NT 29)** — cast 771 CE, ~19 tons, world's largest extant bronze bells
**2025 special exhibition** reunited all 6 Silla gold crowns for first time in 104 years (APEC commemoration)
5 main halls + outdoor sculpture garden with the Emille Bell belfry
**FREE admission**, hours Tue–Sun 10:00–18:00
Closed Mondays — route avoids Mondays

**Wolji (Anapji) Pond Hall** — 33,000+ artifacts excavated 1975–1976 when the Silla royal pond was drained for restoration; rare wooden objects preserved by the anaerobic mud, including roof tiles, lacquered furniture fragments, and dice from royal drinking games
timeUsed: Silla History Hall + Silla Art Hall (~30 min)
Wolji (Anapji) Hall + outdoor Emille Bell (~25 min)
Cheonmachong Gold Crown room dwell + photography (~10 min)
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: The museum is the natural sequel to Bulguksa — guests just saw the temple's National Treasures in situ, and now see the parallel artifacts (gold crowns, bells, Wolji palace treasures) excavated from the same Silla heartland. The calm-pace framing makes this a 75-min visit (vs. 60 min in the fixed-itinerary sister tour), letting history lovers actually read the bilingual labels. Free admission also makes the longer dwell zero-cost from a budget perspective. The Cheonmachong Gold Crown room is the most-photographed gallery — guests should plan ~10 minutes there alone within the 75-min dwell.

sources: National Museum of Korea (Gyeongju Branch official)

itineraryStops.name: 교촌한옥마을
itineraryStops.category: Hanok heritage village — Gyeongju Choi Clan
itineraryStops.description: 경주 최씨 가문의 300년 전통 한옥 고택 마을. 한복 체험, 교동법주(전통 쌀막걸리) 시음 가능. 월정교까지 도보 5분. 방문 시간: 약 60분.
highlights: **Gyeongju Choi Clan: 12 generations of continuous wealth + ethical-merchant code**
**House of Choi Clan = Important Folklore Material No. 27**
**Gyodong Beopju (교동법주) liquor = Important Intangible Cultural Asset No. 86-3** — 350-year recipe
**Yoseokgung site** — residence of 7th-c. Silla Princess Yoseok (mother of Idu-script scholar Seol Chong)
Within 15-min walk: Gyerim Forest + Naemulwangneung tomb + Gyeongjuhyanggyo Confucian school
**FREE admission** for the village; ₩2,000–3,000 for interior house tours
Open year-round; cherry blossoms in early April + golden ginkgos in late October
**60-min dwell** allows for slow village walk + Beopju tasting

**Princess Yoseok was the mother of Seol Chong** (~660 CE), creator of the **Idu writing system** that adapted Chinese characters to Korean grammar — the first major Korean linguistic invention, predating Hangul by 800 years
timeUsed: Walk through main lane + Choi Clan House courtyard (~20 min)
Yoseokgung site + adjacent ginkgo + cherry trees (~15 min)
Free time + return to coach (~15 min)
Gyerim Forest + Naemulwangneung tomb walk within 15-min radius (~10 min)
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Gyochon brings the day from imperial-grand (Bulguksa, museum) to intimate domestic-historical scale — the same Silla civilization seen from the merchant-class side rather than the royal/Buddhist side. The 60-min dwell is **15 min longer** than the UNESCO-Legacy sister tour, calibrated to let calm-pace guests actually browse the Beopju shop and walk the surrounding ancient sites without watching the clock. The 60-min dwell is also the day's most relaxed pace — guests can buy Beopju as a take-home souvenir (boxed bottles available at the family residence shop) rather than rushing through the tasting.

sources: Korea Tourism Organization (Gyeongju Choi Clan + Gyochon Hanok Village)
Cultural Heritage Administration (Important Folklore Material No. 27 designation)

itineraryStops.name: 월정교
itineraryStops.category: Reconstructed Silla wooden bridge — Korea's largest
itineraryStops.description: 760년 통일신라 목교를 2013년 복원한 한국 최대 목교(66.57m). 일몰 후 조명 점등이 경주 최고 야경. 황리단길 도보 5분 거리. 무료 입장. 방문 시간: 약 30분.
highlights: **Korea's largest wooden bridge** — 60.57 m × 13 m, covered span on 4 stone piers
Originally built **760 CE under King Gyeongdeok** (35th Silla king) per Samguk Sagi
Lost during Joseon era → excavated **1984–1986** → reconstructed **2008–2013** + gatehouses **2016–2018**
**Reopened April 2018** — 1,258 years after original construction
**Historic Site (Sajeok)** designated November 27, 2004
**'100 Best Tourist Attractions in Korea 2025–2026'** — Ministry of Culture, Sports & Tourism
Spans Namcheon River, ~10-min drive from Gyochon
**FREE admission, 24h open** — daylight wood-detail viewing on this tour

**Reconstruction methodology used traditional Korean wooden joinery (Hanok craft technique)** without modern fasteners — based on archaeological evidence from the 1984–1986 excavation that recovered **original 8th-century timber fragments preserved in the Namcheon riverbed silt**
timeUsed: Coach drop-off + walk to bridge (~5 min)
Cross the bridge + photo time (~15 min)
River-edge view of full span (~7 min)
Gatehouse interpretive exhibit on reconstruction archaeology + Hanok joinery (~5 min)
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Woljeonggyo is the day's quiet closing note — a single beautiful piece of Silla architecture on a calm river, perfect after the visual density of Bulguksa + the museum + the hanok village. Calm-pace guests appreciate that this final stop **doesn't require any climbing or walking distance** and offers easy wide-angle photo opportunities. The afternoon-light arrival also avoids the heavily Instagrammed sunset crowd. The bridge also has a small **interpretive exhibit at the gatehouse** explaining the reconstruction archaeology and traditional Korean wooden joinery techniques used in the 2008–2018 rebuild.

sources: Korea Heritage Service (Woljeonggyo Reconstruction Project archive)

itineraryStops.name: 부산 귀환
itineraryStops.description: 경부고속도로로 부산 귀환(약 75분). 해운대 → 서면 → 부산역 역순 하차. 유네스코 레거시 투어보다 더 일찍 도착합니다.
highlights: Reverse-sequence drop-off: Haeundae → Seomyeon → Busan Station
Drop-off completed by ~18:30
Dinner-friendly arrival time for Busan evening plans
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Reverse pickup sequence is the most time-efficient drop-off pattern.

routeFlowStops.name: Ahopsan Bamboo Forest (or Haeundae Dalmaji Hill in cherry season)

routePhases.description: 부산 지하철 3개 역 순차 픽업(08:10, 08:30, 09:10) 후 아홉산숲으로 이동.
아홉산에서 불국사까지 약 70분 이동 후 60분 사찰 탐방.
경주 현지 점심(60분) 후 국립경주박물관 하이라이트 60분 관람.
경주에서 경부고속도로로 부산 귀환. 해운대·서면·부산역 하차.

routeShapeIntro.title: How this day flows
routeShapeIntro.subtitle: A calm, year-round version of the Gyeongju heritage circuit that prioritizes architectural depth over stop count. Where many Gyeongju day tours pack in 7–8 sites and feel rushed, this route runs five thoughtful stops with adequate dwell time at each — particularly Bulguksa, the National Museum, and Gyochon Village. The closing Woljeonggyo Bridge stop happens during daylight rather than illumination time, which matches the calm-pace philosophy of the day. Designed for travelers who value time-at-each-site over checkbox sightseeing.

bestFor: Travelers who want Gyeongju's classic stops without rushing
Travelers who prefer earlier evening returns
lessIdealFor: Travelers wanting deep museum time (UNESCO Legacy version is longer)
Travelers needing a tight same-day flight buffer

routeLogicSections.title: Why this route is built this way

items.label: Classic Gyeongju in 10.5 hours, not 11.5
items.detail: Same six attractions as the UNESCO Legacy version (Ahopsan, Bulguksa, lunch, museum, Gyochon, bridge), but each block runs 15–30 minutes shorter. Net result: a slightly less rushed pace inside each stop, and an earlier return (≈18:50 vs ≈19:45). Trade-off: less time in the museum's deeper Wolji galleries — the highlight loop only.

items.label: Reconstructed Silla bridge as a quiet close
items.detail: Woljeonggyo Bridge is short, flat, and photogenic — a 30-minute final stop after the museum and Gyochon. It's illuminated nightly from sunset to ≈22:00; on this route the bridge is reached at 16:30, which is daylight in spring–autumn and just before/at sunset in November–February. We don't promise night-view in summer because it isn't possible at this stop time.

items.label: Year-round, with one smart seasonal swap
items.detail: Ahopsan Bamboo Forest is replaced by Haeundae Dalmaji Hill during cherry blossom season (mid-March to mid-April). The rest of the day stays identical.

items.label: Mini-van size between coach and private
items.detail: Coach tours from Busan run 35–45 passengers at low per-person prices; private tours run 1–4 passengers at much higher per-group rates. This small-group mini-van format sits between — usually under 10 passengers per departure.

practicalAccordionItems.title: What's included
practicalAccordionItems.preview: Round-trip mini-van transport from Busan, certified English/Chinese-speaking guide,…
content: Round-trip mini-van transport from Busan, certified English/Chinese-speaking guide, fuel/tolls/parking.
Not included: lunch (≈₩15,000–₩25,000), Ahopsan Bamboo Forest admission (₩5,000, paid on site), tips.
All other site admissions are free: Bulguksa (free since May 2023), Gyeongju National Museum (free since 2008), Gyochon Hanok Village, Woljeonggyo Bridge.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Pickup & drop-off
practicalAccordionItems.preview: Three sequential pickup points along Busan Subway Line 1: Busan Station Exit 4 (08:10),…
content: Three sequential pickup points along Busan Subway Line 1: Busan Station Exit 4 (08:10), Seomyeon Station Exit 4 (08:30), Haeundae Station Exit 5 (09:10).
Drop-offs in reverse order: Haeundae ≈18:15, Seomyeon ≈18:35, Busan Station ≈18:50.
Provide a WhatsApp / KakaoTalk number at booking; the guide will message the day before with vehicle details.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Walking & terrain
content: Total walking distance ≈3.5–4 km across the day — slightly less than the UNESCO Legacy version because the museum block is shorter.
Bulguksa has stone steps between terraces; the museum is flat indoor walking; Gyochon and the bridge are flat.
Comfortable closed-toe shoes essential — Bulguksa stone is uneven.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Cancellation
practicalAccordionItems.preview: Free cancellation up to 24 hours before pickup.
content: Free cancellation up to 24 hours before pickup.
Same-day cancellations and no-shows are non-refundable.
Operator-cancellation (force majeure, weather, road closure): full refund.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Seasonal route notes
practicalAccordionItems.preview: Cherry blossom season (mid-March to mid-April; exact dates vary year-to-year): Ahopsan…
content: Cherry blossom season (mid-March to mid-April; exact dates vary year-to-year): Ahopsan Bamboo Forest is replaced by Haeundae Dalmaji Hill, Busan's coastal cherry-tunnel road.
Winter (November–February): Woljeonggyo Bridge is reached at 16:30, around or just after sunset — the only window when the post-sunset reflection shot is reliable.

practicalWeatherStatic._seasonalSummary: Gyeongju is inland southeast Korea — winters slightly colder than coastal Busan, summers hotter. Spring and autumn are the most comfortable seasons.

seasonalVariations.season: Spring (mid-Mar to mid-Apr, cherry blossom window)
seasonalVariations.description: Ahopsan opener swaps for Haeundae Dalmaji Hill cherry-blossom road. Bulguksa grounds peak in mid-April.
Bamboo forest stays cool; museum air-conditioned. Bulguksa's mountain setting milder than coastal Busan. Bridge in full daylight at 16:30.
Bulguksa's late-October foliage is one of Korea's classic photographic moments. Bridge at 16:30 still mostly daylight.
Quietest crowds; museum becomes the most-valued indoor stop. Sunset 17:15–17:30 — the bridge begins to illuminate near the end of your visit.

bookingTrustItems.description: Authorized Korean tour operator with full insurance
bookingTrustItems.body: 유네스코 레거시 투어와 동일한 경주 코스를 운영하는 공인 현지 운영사.

bookingTrustItems.title: 코스 전문
bookingTrustItems.description: Hand-tuned itinerary, not a generic loop
bookingTrustItems.body: 수백 차례 부산-경주 루프를 정제해온 코스 전문가들이 운영합니다.

bookingTrustItems.title: 그룹 규모 관리
bookingTrustItems.body: 최대 10인 미니밴으로 불국사 국보 6점 탐방을 대형 단체 대기줄 없이 이동합니다.

staticQuestions.question: '유네스코 레거시 투어(부산 출발)'와 어떻게 다른가요?
staticQuestions.answer: 두 투어 모두 아홉산 → 불국사 → 박물관 → 교촌 → 월정교 동일 코스입니다. '고도 투어'는 페이스가 더 여유롭고 부산에 더 일찍 귀환합니다. '유네스코 레거시'는 박물관 체류 시간이 더 길고 심층 가이드가 제공됩니다.

staticQuestions.question: 월정교 야간 조명을 볼 수 있나요?
staticQuestions.answer: 이 투어에서는 대부분의 경우 어렵습니다. 월정교는 일몰 후 조명이 켜지지만, 이 코스는 일몰 전 월정교에 도착합니다.

staticQuestions.question: 석굴암은 왜 포함되지 않나요?
staticQuestions.answer: 석굴암은 불국사와 같은 유네스코 등재이지만 산 위 8km 거리에 위치, 좁은 주차와 긴 이동 시간으로 당일 일정에 포함하기 어렵습니다.

staticQuestions.question: 입장료가 포함되어 있나요?
staticQuestions.answer: 아홉산숲(₩5,000, 현장 지불)만 별도입니다. 불국사(2023년 5월부터 무료), 국립경주박물관·교촌·월정교는 무료입니다.

staticQuestions.question: 박물관 내 영어 안내가 있나요?
staticQuestions.answer: 네, 3개 상설전시관 전체에 영어·중국어 전시 패널이 있습니다.

staticQuestions.question: 불국사에서 사진을 찍을 수 있나요?
staticQuestions.answer: 네, 경내 전체에서 가능합니다. 플래시와 삼각대는 피해 주세요.

staticQuestions.question: 채식 점심 옵션이 있나요?
staticQuestions.answer: 네, 경주 쌈밥은 채소 반찬 중심이라 고기 없이도 즐길 수 있습니다. 가이드가 안내해 드립니다.

staticQuestions.question: 비가 오면 어떻게 되나요?
staticQuestions.answer: 가벼운 비: 예정대로 진행. 강우 시: 불국사 계단 미끄럼 주의, 아홉산 길 진흙 상태 가능. 박물관·교촌은 대부분 진행 가능.

staticQuestions.question: 경주를 처음 방문하는 분에게 적합한가요?
staticQuestions.answer: 네, 경주 핵심 명소를 여유 있는 페이스로 방문할 수 있습니다. 유네스코 문화유산을 원하는 첫 방문자에게 적합합니다.

staticQuestions.question: 도보 이동이 얼마나 되나요?
staticQuestions.answer: 약 3.5~4km. 불국사가 가장 활동적인 구간(고르지 않은 석재). 박물관은 실내 평지. 교촌·월정교는 가볍습니다.

staticQuestions.question: 가족 여행에 적합한가요?
staticQuestions.answer: 만 8세 이상, 하루 일정을 소화할 수 있는 어린이에게 적합합니다.

staticQuestions.question: 어르신도 참여할 수 있나요?
staticQuestions.answer: 3.5~4km 도보와 불국사 고르지 않은 석재를 소화할 수 있는 활동적인 어르신께 권장합니다.

staticQuestions.question: 권장 최소 연령은 몇 세인가요?
staticQuestions.answer: 만 8세 이상, 하루 페이스와 도보 활동이 가능한 어린이에게 적합합니다.

price.priceNote: Small-group price not yet confirmed from operator listing. Comparable Gyeongju-from-Busan tours range $54–90 across packages and operators. Once confirmed, AtoC Korea sale price applies a flat 10% discount.

pickup_dropoff.notes: Same six-attraction route year-round (Bulguksa, museum, Gyochon, bridge, plus Ahopsan Bamboo Forest as morning opener). Cherry blossom season (mid-March to mid-April) swaps Ahopsan for Haeundae Dalmaji Hill. Choose the pickup exit closest to your hotel.

walking_notes: Moderate walking with some uneven surfaces.
keywords: from busan gyeongju ancient capital day tour
synonym_hints: from busan gyeongju ancient capital day tour
matching_profile.signature_window_label: Year-round (cherry blossom & ginkgo seasonal anchors at Gyochon)

matching_metadata.differentiation_vs_unesco_legacy_sister: Same 5 substantive POIs but **15-min longer dwell at each substantive stop** + **lunch is 60 min vs. 45 min** + framing emphasizes 'calm pace' and 'history lover' vs. sister tour's 'UNESCO Legacy + national museum' positioning. Both routes appropriate for different guest temperaments.
matching_metadata.patch_target: 15 substantive stops across 3 tours; Batch 13a/13b authoring → A-grade pv=6 PASS

props.headlineLine2: Gyeongju's six classic stops in a 10.5-hour day from Busan

hero.tagline: Bulguksa, the museum, Gyochon, the bridge — calmer pace, earlier return

glanceItems.label: Group
glanceItems.value: Small group, mini-van (typically up to ≈10 passengers)

glanceItems.label: Pickup
glanceItems.value: Busan Station Exit 4 (08:10), Seomyeon Exit 4 (08:30), Haeundae Exit 5 (09:10)

glanceItems.label: Best season
glanceItems.value: Year-round; cherry-blossom alt route mid-Mar to mid-Apr

glanceItems.label: Walking
glanceItems.value: Moderate — ≈3.5–4 km. Calmer pace than the UNESCO Legacy version.

glanceItems.label: Includes
glanceItems.value: Round-trip transport, certified guide. Bulguksa, museum, Gyochon, bridge: free. Only Ahopsan (₩5,000) and lunch are paid on-day.

galleryItems.alt: Stone bridges and Dabotap pagoda at Bulguksa Temple in afternoon light
galleryItems.caption: Bulguksa — UNESCO World Heritage. Two pagodas (Dabotap, Seokgatap) are National Treasures No. 20 and 21.

galleryItems.alt: Tile-roofed hanok houses along a quiet alley in Gyochon village
galleryItems.caption: Gyochon Hanok Village — the Choi family compound and Gyeongju Hyanggyo Confucian school.

galleryItems.alt: Light filtering through dense bamboo stalks along a forest path
galleryItems.caption: Ahopsan Bamboo Forest — privately preserved by the Mun clan since 1638.

galleryItems.alt: Silla gold artifacts displayed under museum case lighting
galleryItems.caption: Gyeongju National Museum — Silla artifacts including the Cheonmachong gold crown.

galleryItems.alt: Wooden bridge pavilion reflected in calm river water
galleryItems.caption: Woljeonggyo Bridge — 66-meter Silla-style reconstruction completed in 2018.

itineraryStops.name: Busan pickup (3 subway stations)
itineraryStops.description: Sequential pickup along Busan Subway Line 1: Busan Station Exit 4 (08:10), Seomyeon Station Exit 4 (08:30), Haeundae Station Exit 5 (09:10). Choose the exit closest to your hotel.
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Three pickup options across central Busan reduce taxi cost.

itineraryStops.name: Ahopsan Bamboo Forest
itineraryStops.description: Privately preserved bamboo grove inside the Nampyeong Mun clan's ancestral estate in Gijang-gun, Busan — the family has owned and maintained the forest since 1638. Several Korean dramas have filmed here, including the bamboo-corridor scenes in 'Mr. Sunshine' (2018). The walking loop is roughly 1 km on flat compacted dirt, takes 45–60 minutes at a slow pace, and stays cool in summer because the canopy filters most direct sunlight. Adult admission ₩5,000 (cash; not included in tour price). Spring (cherry blossom season) is the only exception when this stop swaps for Haeundae Dalmaji Hill.
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Calm, cool sensory opener between Busan and Gyeongju.

itineraryStops.name: Bulguksa Temple (UNESCO World Heritage)
itineraryStops.description: Inscribed by UNESCO together with Seokguram Grotto in December 1995. The temple was founded in 528 CE under Silla King Beopheung, expanded in 751 CE by chief minister Kim Daeseong, burned in the 1592–98 Imjin War, and progressively restored from 1920 onward; current wooden structures were largely rebuilt in 1973 around the original stone foundations. The two pagodas in the main courtyard — ornate Dabotap and austere Seokgatap, both 8th-century — are National Treasures No. 20 and No. 21. The two stone bridges at the lower terrace, Cheongun-gyo and Baegun-gyo, each have 33 steps representing the 33 heavens of Buddhist cosmology. Admission made FREE in May 2023 (parking ₩1,000–₩2,000). 60 minutes covers the main courtyard and pagodas at a relaxed pace.
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: The architectural anchor of any Gyeongju day; UNESCO-listed.

itineraryStops.name: Lunch (Gyeongju local restaurant)
itineraryStops.description: Sit-down restaurant near Bulguksa with English/Chinese menus. Typical: Gyeongju ssambap (banchan-heavy rice-and-leaf wraps, the regional signature, ≈₩15,000–₩20,000), bulgogi set, or vegetarian banchan platter on request. Lunch paid on the day; budget ₩15,000–₩25,000 per person.
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Gyeongju ssambap is the signature regional dish.

itineraryStops.name: Gyeongju National Museum
itineraryStops.description: Korea's primary repository of Silla-era artifacts, free admission since November 2008. Three permanent halls: Silla History, Silla Art, and Wolji Hall (artifacts from the Wolji palace pond). The two single objects most visitors come for: the Cheonmachong gold crown (National Treasure No. 188) and the Sacred Bell of Great King Seongdeok (National Treasure No. 29, cast 771 CE, 18.9 metric tons — the largest existing Korean bell, displayed outdoors near the entrance). English and Chinese exhibit panels throughout. Closed Mondays. 60 minutes is enough for the highlight loop; visitors who want more time should book the UNESCO Legacy version.
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Pairs the architectural site with its archaeological contents.

itineraryStops.name: Gyochon Hanok Village
itineraryStops.description: Restored Joseon-era hanok district built around the ancestral home of the Gyeongju Choi family — the 'Choi the Wealthy' clan, famous in Korean civic memory for their twelve-generation rules of conduct (never let neighbors starve, never hold land beyond a 100-ri radius, never accept rent tenants couldn't afford). The Choi residence is preserved as a small museum (free entry); surrounding alleys hold tea houses and the Gyeongju Hyanggyo (Confucian school, current buildings from 1492). Flat hanok lanes; stroller-friendly.
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Flat, easy stop between the museum and the bridge close.

itineraryStops.name: Woljeonggyo Bridge
itineraryStops.description: Wooden bridge crossing the Namcheon stream beside Gyochon. The original Silla bridge stood here from 760 CE until destroyed during the Mongol invasions of the 13th century. The current 66-meter reconstruction was completed in 2018 after archaeological excavations begun in 1986; it has two-story gate pavilions on each end and is illuminated nightly from sunset until ≈22:00. **At the 16:30 visit time the bridge is in daylight in spring–autumn.** The post-sunset reflection shot promoted in many photos is reliably possible only in November–February when sunset is between 17:15 and 17:30. Free entry. 30 minutes covers the bridge crossing and both reflection viewpoints from the riverbank.
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Quiet, low-walking close to the day; 5 minutes' walk from Gyochon.

itineraryStops.name: Return drive to Busan
itineraryStops.description: ≈80 km / 75 min on the Gyeongbu Expressway in normal traffic; up to 90 min in Friday-evening or Sunday-return traffic. Drop-off in reverse pickup order: Haeundae Station Exit 5 ≈18:15, Seomyeon Station Exit 4 ≈18:35, Busan Station Exit 4 ≈18:50.

routeFlowStops.name: Ahopsan Bamboo Forest (or Haeundae Dalmaji Hill in cherry season)

routePhases.description: Pickup at three Busan subway exits (08:10, 08:30, 09:10), then drive ≈30 min to Ahopsan Bamboo Forest in Gijang.
Drive ≈70 min from Ahopsan to Bulguksa, then 60 min walking the temple at a relaxed pace.
Local Gyeongju lunch (60 min), then 60 min in the National Museum's highlight galleries.
Gyochon Hanok Village walk (60 min) and 30 min at Woljeonggyo Bridge.
Gyeongju to Busan via Gyeongbu Expressway. Drop-offs at Haeundae, Seomyeon, Busan Station.

routeShapeIntro.title: How this day flows
routeShapeIntro.subtitle: A calm, year-round version of the Gyeongju heritage circuit that prioritizes architectural depth over stop count. Where many Gyeongju day tours pack in 7–8 sites and feel rushed, this route runs five thoughtful stops with adequate dwell time at each — particularly Bulguksa, the National Museum, and Gyochon Village. The closing Woljeonggyo Bridge stop happens during daylight rather than illumination time, which matches the calm-pace philosophy of the day. Designed for travelers who value time-at-each-site over checkbox sightseeing.

bestFor: Travelers who want Gyeongju's classic stops without rushing
Travelers who prefer earlier evening returns
lessIdealFor: Travelers wanting deep museum time (UNESCO Legacy version is longer)
Travelers needing a tight same-day flight buffer

routeLogicSections.title: Why this route is built this way

items.label: Classic Gyeongju in 10.5 hours, not 11.5
items.detail: Same six attractions as the UNESCO Legacy version (Ahopsan, Bulguksa, lunch, museum, Gyochon, bridge), but each block runs 15–30 minutes shorter. Net result: a slightly less rushed pace inside each stop, and an earlier return (≈18:50 vs ≈19:45). Trade-off: less time in the museum's deeper Wolji galleries — the highlight loop only.

items.label: Reconstructed Silla bridge as a quiet close
items.detail: Woljeonggyo Bridge is short, flat, and photogenic — a 30-minute final stop after the museum and Gyochon. It's illuminated nightly from sunset to ≈22:00; on this route the bridge is reached at 16:30, which is daylight in spring–autumn and just before/at sunset in November–February. We don't promise night-view in summer because it isn't possible at this stop time.

items.label: Year-round, with one smart seasonal swap
items.detail: Ahopsan Bamboo Forest is replaced by Haeundae Dalmaji Hill during cherry blossom season (mid-March to mid-April). The rest of the day stays identical.

items.label: Mini-van size between coach and private
items.detail: Coach tours from Busan run 35–45 passengers at low per-person prices; private tours run 1–4 passengers at much higher per-group rates. This small-group mini-van format sits between — usually under 10 passengers per departure.

practicalAccordionItems.title: What's included
practicalAccordionItems.content: Round-trip mini-van transport from Busan, certified English/Chinese-speaking guide, fuel/tolls/parking. Not included: lunch (≈₩15,000–₩25,000), Ahopsan Bamboo Forest admission (₩5,000, paid on site), tips. All other site admissions are free: Bulguksa (free since May 2023), Gyeongju National Museum (free since 2008), Gyochon Hanok Village, Woljeonggyo Bridge.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Pickup & drop-off
practicalAccordionItems.content: Three sequential pickup points along Busan Subway Line 1: Busan Station Exit 4 (08:10), Seomyeon Station Exit 4 (08:30), Haeundae Station Exit 5 (09:10). Drop-offs in reverse order: Haeundae ≈18:15, Seomyeon ≈18:35, Busan Station ≈18:50. Provide a WhatsApp / KakaoTalk number at booking; the guide will message the day before with vehicle details.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Walking & terrain
practicalAccordionItems.content: Moderate. Total walking distance ≈3.5–4 km across the day — slightly less than the UNESCO Legacy version because the museum block is shorter. Bulguksa has stone steps between terraces; the museum is flat indoor walking; Gyochon and the bridge are flat. Comfortable closed-toe shoes essential — Bulguksa stone is uneven.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Cancellation
practicalAccordionItems.content: Free cancellation up to 24 hours before pickup. Same-day cancellations and no-shows are non-refundable. Operator-cancellation (force majeure, weather, road closure): full refund.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Seasonal route notes
practicalAccordionItems.content: Cherry blossom season (mid-March to mid-April; exact dates vary year-to-year): Ahopsan Bamboo Forest is replaced by Haeundae Dalmaji Hill, Busan's coastal cherry-tunnel road. Winter (November–February): Woljeonggyo Bridge is reached at 16:30, around or just after sunset — the only window when the post-sunset reflection shot is reliable.

practicalWeatherStatic._seasonalSummary: Gyeongju is inland southeast Korea — winters slightly colder than coastal Busan, summers hotter. Spring and autumn are the most comfortable seasons.

seasonalVariations.season: Spring (mid-Mar to mid-Apr, cherry blossom window)
seasonalVariations.highlight: Ahopsan opener swaps for Haeundae Dalmaji Hill cherry-blossom road. Bulguksa grounds peak in mid-April.
Bamboo forest stays cool; museum air-conditioned. Bulguksa's mountain setting milder than coastal Busan. Bridge in full daylight at 16:30.
Bulguksa's late-October foliage is one of Korea's classic photographic moments. Bridge at 16:30 still mostly daylight.
Quietest crowds; museum becomes the most-valued indoor stop. Sunset 17:15–17:30 — the bridge begins to illuminate near the end of your visit.

bookingTrustItems.title: Licensed local operator
bookingTrustItems.description: Authorized Korean tour operator with full insurance

bookingTrustItems.title: Route specialists
bookingTrustItems.description: Hand-tuned itinerary, not a generic loop

bookingSupportSteps.title: 12-Hour Reminder
bookingSupportSteps.detail: Weather update and any route adjustments

bookingSupportSteps.title: Final Pickup Info
bookingSupportSteps.detail: Exact pickup time and driver contact

bookingSupportSteps.title: Day-of Route Notes
bookingSupportSteps.detail: Morning briefing based on live conditions

staticQuestions.question: How is this different from the 'UNESCO Legacy' day tour from Busan?
staticQuestions.answer: Both tours run on essentially the same route (Ahopsan → Bulguksa → museum → Gyochon → bridge). The 'Ancient Capital' framing on this product runs each block 15–30 min shorter, returns ≈1 hour earlier (≈18:50 vs ≈19:45), and gives a slightly less rushed pace at each stop in exchange for less depth at the museum. The 'UNESCO Legacy' version emphasizes museum time and heritage interpretation. Choose this version if you prefer earlier returns and a calmer day; choose UNESCO Legacy if you want longer time inside the museum.

staticQuestions.question: Will we see the bridge illuminated?
staticQuestions.answer: Generally no on this tour. The bridge is illuminated nightly year-round from sunset to ≈22:00, but this route reaches it at 16:30 — daylight in spring–autumn. The only window when illumination is reliable at the visit time is November–February, when sunset is between 17:15 and 17:30 and the lights begin coming on toward the end of your stop. If the post-sunset reflection shot is your priority, the UNESCO Legacy tour (which reaches the bridge at 17:00) is a closer fit, or visit independently after sunset.

staticQuestions.question: Why no Seokguram Grotto?
staticQuestions.answer: Seokguram is on the same UNESCO inscription as Bulguksa but sits 8 km up the mountain by a steep narrow road, with parking constraints and a 10-minute walk from the lot. The combination makes it impractical to add to a day that also includes the museum, Gyochon, and the bridge. Most one-day Gyeongju tours include Bulguksa or Seokguram, not both.

staticQuestions.question: Are admissions included?
staticQuestions.answer: Most are free; the only out-of-pocket admission is Ahopsan Bamboo Forest (₩5,000 cash, paid on-site). Bulguksa Temple is free since May 2023; Gyeongju National Museum is free since November 2008; Gyochon Hanok Village and Woljeonggyo Bridge are free. Plan ₩15,000–₩25,000 for lunch + ₩5,000 for Ahopsan, so total typical out-of-pocket ≈ ₩20,000–₩30,000 per person.

staticQuestions.question: Is there English signage in the museum?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes. The Gyeongju National Museum has English and Chinese exhibit panels throughout the three permanent halls. The Sacred Bell at the entrance has multilingual panels. Free English audio guides are available at the information desk with ID deposit.

staticQuestions.question: Can I take photos at Bulguksa?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes throughout the temple grounds, including the two pagodas and lower-terrace stone bridges. Avoid flash and tripods inside the worship halls where services may be in progress. Cherry blossoms peak in mid-April; autumn foliage in late October — the most-photographed times.

staticQuestions.question: Are there vegetarian options for lunch?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes. Gyeongju ssambap is naturally vegetable-heavy; ordering it without meat banchan is straightforward. The guide can also route to a temple-cuisine (사찰음식) restaurant for fully vegetarian meals on request — please advise at booking.

staticQuestions.question: What if it rains?
staticQuestions.answer: Light rain: tour operates as planned. Heavy rain: Bulguksa stone steps become slippery and Ahopsan paths can muddy; the guide may shorten outdoor time and extend the museum (covered, climate-controlled). Typhoon-level: rebooking or full refund.

staticQuestions.question: Is this a good choice for first-time visitors to Gyeongju?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes — it covers Gyeongju's classic stops at a calmer pace than longer tours. First-time visitors who want UNESCO heritage content as part of a Korea trip and prefer earlier evening returns are the primary audience.

staticQuestions.question: How much walking is there?
staticQuestions.answer: ≈3.5–4 km. Bulguksa is the most active (uneven stone, gentle uphill); museum is flat indoor walking; Gyochon and bridge are flat. Comfortable closed-toe shoes essential.

staticQuestions.question: Is this suitable for families?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes for school-age children (≈8+) who can manage a full-day rhythm. The museum's gold crown and the giant bell are typically the kid-favorite stops; the bamboo forest is sensory-friendly. Younger children may flag during the museum visit.

staticQuestions.question: Can older adults join comfortably?
staticQuestions.answer: Recommended for active seniors comfortable with 3.5–4 km of walking and uneven stone at Bulguksa. Pace is moderate with seated museum time built in. The earlier return (≈18:50) makes it easier than longer Gyeongju tours.

staticQuestions.question: What's the recommended minimum age?
staticQuestions.answer: Best fit is around age 8 and up. Children comfortable with the walking note above and a full-day pace; younger may struggle with the museum's quiet halls and the rhythm of a 10.5-hour day.

v17_schema_unification.description: Phase 1: Converted matching_profile scores from 1-5 int scale to 0-1 float scale to match the 3 Step 3 new tours. Mapping 1→0.20, 2→0.40, 3→0.60, 4→0.80, 5→1.00. Mechanical conversion preserves ordinal information; verified-fact recalibration happens in subsequent enhancement batches.
